item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I come back to this article every once in a while.

Thanks for writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of scary articles on this site, but for me this is probably the scariest.

I vaguely remember fearing something like this when I was a child. Upon entering a room, I'd think "what if I could never ever leave this room again?" and eventually came to the conclusion that as long as the trap room was a bathroom I'd be okay, because at least there'd be water. Over the years, I kind of forgot about that fear… at least, until I read this.

You did a really great job. The logs, especially the last one, made me so sad. Upvoted.

item.title: square root of infinity times 0 + -1!
item.content:encoded:
This is simple amazing, the idea of an average family home suddenly trapping a kid in the bathroom is is crazy. I assume teleporting and de-existing the kid out of the bathroom has failed as well, but that kid will soon die of the Foundation will ask the All-Mighty to see if the family got his house by mistake.

btw the title is the fraction and reality code to re-set any lock in the SCP multi-verse, not hyper verse though?
It means a lot to me that this SCP reached this milestone. Thank you all for reading and voting!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had initially upvoted this, however, upon a reread I find it to be wearing out its welcome. The interview logs start strong and have their moments throughout, but the penultimate and final logs are just communicating the same information as before. The dialogue is crunchy and well-written for the most part but the freakouts don't track for me. I think it's reasonable that the kid and his parents would be flipping out but they seem to become desperate and hopeless strangely quickly.

There's no sense of humor or laughing at how ridiculous it is to be trapped in the bathroom like this, which I feel would happen in a real family. Even one with strained relationships. It's just overwhelmingly grim and that takes away from the realism present in the situation, especially in the beginning.

The tasting terrible line is a good one to end on. Overall, given how much this leans on the logs the fact that they're uneven and lose all steam about halfway through makes me feel the need to downvote. Not because it's bad, but because the problems it has are fixable and I hope it encourages them to be fixed. The second and last log is primarily communicating information the reader has already learned and it feels unnecessarily grimdark as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is done brilliantly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good idea, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe you should mention the floor of the bathroom being indestructible as well. Just a thought. Otherwise it would be possible to Shawshank your way out eventually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like a Twilight Zone episode with no tidy, satisfying ending. Just… something unexplainable happens, and keeps happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm inclined to agree with this. The dialogue felt stilted and melodramatic (trying to cram in exposition about this whole family structure, and the psychological decline of the trapped guy, but I think the fact that all of this takes place over 2 days makes it faintly absurd - what I mean here is not that it wouldn't be distressing to be trapped in a room for 2 days, but that it's not really SCP-level-distress, if that makes any sense - people are trapped in spaces for 2 days all over the world not infrequently).

The sad people trapped in anomaly stories at least have going for them some kind of disturbing setting, generally. You could argue that being trapped in a bathroom is also disturbing because it's common and relatable, but again, coming back to this happening over 2 days, it's not that hard for the average person to imagine this experience, whether being stuck in a lift, in a cave, or even in a bathroom for mundane, non-supernatural reasons, which returns to the notion of being 'not really SCP-level'. I kind of got the same vibes from this as I would from some heart-rending daytime TV special about a little kid who fell into a well. As harsh as this sounds, the entire dialogue could have been about Timmy falling into the well by just changing the lines from being unable to break the door down to being unable to pull him out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my first read since returning, and it's alright.

I definitely don't really have much of an emotional connection to the SCP, as I personally have never thought of being stuck in a bathroom or other place like so (other than maybe a prison? hell, I wouldn't know, I don't usually get homesick or claustrophobic), so this didn't really hit home to me as well as for other people. I do like the fact he's in some sort of prison, and that being a prison he's used to, being his own bathroom; he feels so alien to a place he's been in before. Sucks that this kid is stuck in there, most likely being forced to eat MRE's until he turns old. Simple but it does make you feel bad for the guy.

It's definitely good writing though. The clinical tone was executed well, and the logs didn't drag on forever and actually made me curious how the situation would unfold. For now it's a tentative no-vote, but I will probably decide on a vote when I get more accustomed to such situations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why did I never try that, damn. Change made, thank ye

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
reminds me too much of other SCPs for me to really enjoy (3001 and that paper towel dispenser one), and i didn't feel all that much emotion from it, which surprised me since the character that is falling victim to this anomaly is young, and i usually feel connections when young people are in danger.

instead, i just ended up feeling bored halfway. the dialogue was tedious to get through, and you could predict everything that could happen due to the description. im all for simple SCPs but this was weak. i think the "person trapped inside anomaly, can't get out and gets sad" shtick is getting really old.

1-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changing the "width=250px" to "width=225px" seems to do it; at least in the preview. It works for all the images, and like, allows the images to be flicked through; but I haven't tried committing the change so I don't know if that causes a change that breaks it? (I would make the edit myself but it feels like it's a stylistic change that should be left up to you?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the picture box feels like it should be narrowed to get rid of the vertical white bars on either side of the photos?

Not possible with the image carousal, sadly (there might be a way but I can't figure it out)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good; with one pretty minor thing: the picture box feels like it should be narrowed to get rid of the vertical white bars on either side of the photos? they feel weird and out of place to me; but this might just be a personal thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck. +1
Very sad. While I think 1831 is more impactful, I still enjoyed the read. It seems like relations/social-focused pieces is a strength of yours.
Jesus, this scares me. Being stuck in a bathroom is an actual fear I have, and this really preys on it. It’s very well-written too. +1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Image stuff
– hide block

All three images are mine, and anyone can use them as they please.

It tastes horrible…

Several weeks ago, I suffered a panic attack at my home. It occurred while I was in the bathroom, and it caused me to believe that I was permanently trapped in the bathroom, without logically thinking that I could just… open the door. While I was fearing for my life, I was also fearing for my family's well being.

This article, while also being a spiritual successor to SCP-1831, is inspired by that event. Here's the music that I listened to while writing this for extra immersion.

Thanks to Henzoid, tupacofficial, Kuolema19 and FloppyPhoenix for looking at this before it went up.
